The drug is intended for use in treating metastatic [[melanoma]].<ref>{{pmid|23724846}}</ref>

'''Pembrolizumab''' (formerly '''lambrolizumab''';<ref>{{cite document | url = http://www.ama-assn.org/resources/doc/usan/pembrolizumab.pdf | title = Statement on a Nonproprietary Name Adopted by the USAN Council | date = November 27, 2013}}</ref>also known as '''MK-3475''') is a drug in development by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]] that targets the [[programmed cell death 1]] (PD-1) receptor. The drug is intended for use in treating metastatic [[melanoma]].<ref>{{cite pmid|23724846}}</ref><ref>[http://www.ynetnews.com/articles/0,7340,L-4527743,00.html article] from a news website (ynetnews.com, June 9, 2014)</ref>

==Uses==
This drug heps the immune system to fight various forms of cancer. The drug disrupt the ability of cancer to hijack the immune system and boosts the immune response to certain cancers. In testing the drug on patients with metastatic melanoma, researchers reported in November that about 9 percent of patients who took the drug had no observable cancer after 12 weeks while half of the study participants had tumor shrinkage.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Winslow|first1=Ron|title=The Future of Medicine Is Now|url=http://online.wsj.com/news/articles/SB10001424127887323530404578205692226506324|website=http://online.wsj.com/}}</ref>

The drug is intended for use in treating metastatic [[melanoma]].<ref>{{cite pmid|23724846}}</ref><ref>[http://www.ynetnews.com/articles/0,7340,L-4527743,00.html article] from a news website (ynetnews.com, June 9, 2014)</ref> Pembrolizumab was invented by '''Gregory Carven''', '''Hans van Eenennaam''' and '''John Dulos''' at Organon Biosciences which later became Schering Plough Research Institute and then Merck & Co. <ref>http://patft.uspto.gov/netacgi/nph-Parser?Sect1=PTO2&Sect2=HITOFF&p=1&u=%2Fnetahtml%2FPTO%2Fsearch-bool.html&r=1&f=G&l=50&co1=AND&d=PTXT&s1=carven.INNM.&OS=IN/carven&RS=IN/carven</ref>

On September 4, 2014 the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved Pembrolizumab as a breakthrough therapy. It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name=FDA20140904>[http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma  FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma]</ref>

'''Pembrolizumab''' (Trade name '''Keytruda''' formerly '''lambrolizumab''';<ref>{{cite document | url = http://www.ama-assn.org/resources/doc/usan/pembrolizumab.pdf | title = Statement on a Nonproprietary Name Adopted by the USAN Council | date = November 27, 2013}}</ref>also known as '''MK-3475''') is a drug marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]] that targets the [[programmed cell death 1]] (PD-1) receptor. The drug is intended for use in treating metastatic [[melanoma]].<ref>{{cite pmid|23724846}}</ref><ref>[http://www.ynetnews.com/articles/0,7340,L-4527743,00.html article] from a news website (ynetnews.com, June 9, 2014)</ref> Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences which later became Schering Plough Research Institute and then Merck & Co. <ref>http://patft.uspto.gov/netacgi/nph-Parser?Sect1=PTO2&Sect2=HITOFF&p=1&u=%2Fnetahtml%2FPTO%2Fsearch-bool.html&r=1&f=G&l=50&co1=AND&d=PTXT&s1=carven.INNM.&OS=IN/carven&RS=IN/carven</ref>

On September 4, 2014 the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved Pembrolizumab as a [[FDA Fast Track Development Program|breakthrough therapy]]. It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name=FDA20140904>[http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma  FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma]</ref>

The drug is now in Phase II trials for non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease at the University of Pennsylvania. <ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer|  "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], February 4, 2015.</ref>

'''Pembrolizumab''' (formerly '''MK-3475''' and '''lambrolizumab''', trade name '''Keytruda'''<ref>{{cite document | url = http://www.ama-assn.org/resources/doc/usan/pembrolizumab.pdf | title = Statement on a Nonproprietary Name Adopted by the USAN Council | date = November 27, 2013}}</ref>) is a drug marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]] that targets the [[programmed cell death 1]] (PD-1) receptor. The drug is intended for use in treating metastatic [[melanoma]].<ref>{{cite pmid|23724846}}</ref><ref>[http://www.ynetnews.com/articles/0,7340,L-4527743,00.html article] from a news website (ynetnews.com, June 9, 2014)</ref> Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences which later became Schering Plough Research Institute and then Merck & Co.<ref>http://patft.uspto.gov/netacgi/nph-Parser?Sect1=PTO2&Sect2=HITOFF&p=1&u=%2Fnetahtml%2FPTO%2Fsearch-bool.html&r=1&f=G&l=50&co1=AND&d=PTXT&s1=carven.INNM.&OS=IN/carven&RS=IN/carven</ref>

On September 4, 2014 the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]]. It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name=FDA20140904>[http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma  FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma]</ref>

The drug is also in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with [[oligometastatic disease]]. <ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], February 4, 2015.</ref>

'''Pembrolizumab''' (formerly '''MK-3475''' and '''lambrolizumab''', trade name '''Keytruda'''<ref>{{cite document | url = http://www.ama-assn.org/resources/doc/usan/pembrolizumab.pdf | title = Statement on a Nonproprietary Name Adopted by the USAN Council | date = November 27, 2013}}</ref>) is a drug marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]] that targets the [[programmed cell death 1]] (PD-1) receptor.<ref name="NYT-20150529">{{cite news |last=Pollack |first=Andrew |title=New Class of Drugs Shows More Promise in Treating Cancer |url=http://www.nytimes.com/2015/05/30/business/new-class-of-drugs-shows-more-promise-in-treating-cancer.html |date=May 29, 2015 |work=[[New York Times]] |accessdate=May 30, 2015 }}</ref> The drug is intended for use in treating metastatic [[melanoma]].<ref>{{cite pmid|23724846}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.ynetnews.com/articles/0,7340,L-4527743,00.html |title=Israeli-American team unveils revolutionary drug in fight against skin cancer |publisher=ynetnews.com |date=June 9, 2014 |accessdate=June 2015 |first=Nechama |last=Duek}}</ref> Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences which later became Schering Plough Research Institute and then Merck & Co.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref>

On September 4, 2014 the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]]. It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name=FDA20140904>{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref>

== Clinical trials ==

A large phase I trial led to response rates of 37-38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date = April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full}}</ref>

The drug is in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], February 4, 2015.</ref>

'''Pembrolizumab''' (formerly '''MK-3475''' and '''lambrolizumab''', trade name '''Keytruda'''<ref>{{cite document | url = http://www.ama-assn.org/resources/doc/usan/pembrolizumab.pdf | title = Statement on a Nonproprietary Name Adopted by the USAN Council | date = November 27, 2013}}</ref>) is a drug marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]] that targets the [[programmed cell death 1]] (PD-1) receptor.<ref name="NYT-20150529">{{cite news |last=Pollack |first=Andrew |title=New Class of Drugs Shows More Promise in Treating Cancer |url=http://www.nytimes.com/2015/05/30/business/new-class-of-drugs-shows-more-promise-in-treating-cancer.html |date=May 29, 2015 |work=[[New York Times]] |accessdate=May 30, 2015 }}</ref> The drug is intended for use in treating metastatic [[melanoma]].<ref>{{cite pmid|23724846}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.ynetnews.com/articles/0,7340,L-4527743,00.html |title=Israeli-American team unveils revolutionary drug in fight against skin cancer |publisher=ynetnews.com |date=June 9, 2014 |accessdate=June 2015 |first=Nechama |last=Duek}}</ref> 

Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences which later became Schering Plough Research Institute and then Merck & Co.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]]. It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name=FDA20140904>{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref>

== Clinical trials ==

A large phase I trial led to response rates of 37-38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date = April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full}}</ref>

The drug is in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], February 4, 2015.</ref>

Recently, the drug has been used to treat former U.S. president Jimmy Carter.<ref>http://nyti.ms/1E6vpZ9</ref>

'''Pembrolizumab''' (formerly '''MK-3475''' and '''lambrolizumab''', trade name '''Keytruda'''<ref>{{cite document | url = http://www.ama-assn.org/resources/doc/usan/pembrolizumab.pdf | title = Statement on a Nonproprietary Name Adopted by the USAN Council | date = November 27, 2013}}</ref>) is a drug marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]] that targets the [[programmed cell death 1]] (PD-1) receptor.<ref name="NYT-20150529">{{cite news |last=Pollack |first=Andrew |title=New Class of Drugs Shows More Promise in Treating Cancer |url=http://www.nytimes.com/2015/05/30/business/new-class-of-drugs-shows-more-promise-in-treating-cancer.html |date=May 29, 2015 |work=[[New York Times]] |accessdate=May 30, 2015 }}</ref> The drug is intended for use in treating metastatic [[melanoma]].<ref>{{cite pmid|23724846}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.ynetnews.com/articles/0,7340,L-4527743,00.html |title=Israeli-American team unveils revolutionary drug in fight against skin cancer |publisher=ynetnews.com |date=June 9, 2014 |accessdate=June 2015 |first=Nechama |last=Duek}}</ref> 

Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences which later became Schering Plough Research Institute and then Merck & Co.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]]. It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name=FDA20140904>{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref>

==Mechanism of action==

Pembrolizumab is a therapeutic antibody that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death receptor]].  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response against the cancer cells.  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid an inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref>. In patients with cancer, antibody blockade against this receptor such as with pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy.|journal=Nature reviews. Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of number of closely related therapies dubbed [[immune checkpoint blockade]].

== Clinical trials ==

A large phase I trial led to response rates of 37-38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date = April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full}}</ref>

The drug is in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], February 4, 2015.</ref>

Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences which later became Schering Plough Research Institute and then Merck & Co.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]]. It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name=FDA20140904>{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref> It is marketed by [[Merck & Co., Inc|Merck]].

On October 2, 2015, the US FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of metastatic non-small cell lung cancer in patients whose tumors express PD-L1 and who have failed treatment with other chemotherapeutic agents. <ref>http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm</ref>

==Mechanism of action==

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]].  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response against cancer cells.  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid an inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In patients with cancer, antibody blockade against this receptor such as with pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy.|journal=Nature reviews. Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[immune checkpoint blockade]].

== Clinical trials ==

A large phase I trial led to response rates of 37-38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date = April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full}}</ref>

The drug is in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], February 4, 2015.</ref>

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name=FDA>{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced melanoma|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm412802.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=September 4, 2014}}</ref> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with [[ipilimumab]] and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name=FDA20140904>{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref> It is marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]].

On October 2, 2015, the US [[FDA]] approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] non-small cell lung cancer in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref>http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm</ref><ref name=FDA2>{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small-cell lung cancer|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=October 2, 2015}}</ref>

==Mechanism of action==

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]].  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response against cancer cells.  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid an inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In patients with cancer, antibody blockade against this receptor such as with pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy.|journal=Nature reviews. Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[immune checkpoint blockade]].

== Clinical trials ==
A large phase I trial led to response rates of 37–38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date=April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full |pmid=25838373 |volume=348 |pages=56–61}}</ref>

The drug is in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], University of Pennsylvania, February 4, 2015.</ref>

== History ==
Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2016.

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced melanoma|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm412802.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=September 4, 2014}}</ref> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904">{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref> It is marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]].

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small cell lung cancer|publisher=}}</ref><ref name="FDA2">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small-cell lung cancer|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=October 2, 2015}}</ref>

Pembrolizumab was priced at $150,000 per year when it launched.<ref name=Fierce>[http://www.fiercepharmamarketing.com/story/amgen-slaps-record-breaking-178k-price-rare-leukemia-drug-blincyto/2014-12-18 Amgen slaps record-breaking $178K price on rare leukemia drug Blincyto]</ref>

==Mechanism of action==

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]].  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response to cancer cells.  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In cancer patients antibody blockade against this receptor such as with Pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy.|journal=Nature reviews. Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[Cancer immunotherapy#Immune checkpoints|checkpoint therapy]].

== Clinical trials ==
As of 2015 a large phase I [[clinical trial]] produced response rates of 37–38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with Ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date=April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full |pmid=25838373 |volume=348 |pages=56–61}}</ref>

Also in 2015 the drug was in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], University of Pennsylvania, February 4, 2015.</ref>

The KEYNOTE-012 phase IB study is testing pembrolizumab for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]].<ref name=Key012-2016-05>[http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727 Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer]</ref> It reported encouraging interim results (eg in TNBC) in 2016.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/> The ongoing phase II KEYNOTE-086 study is evaluating a 200-mg dose of pembrolizumab, given once every 3 weeks, in patients with TNBC.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/>

== History ==
Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced melanoma|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm412802.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=September 4, 2014}}</ref> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904">{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref> It is marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]].

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small cell lung cancer|publisher=}}</ref><ref name="FDA2">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small-cell lung cancer|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=October 2, 2015}}</ref>

Pembrolizumab was priced at $150,000 per year when it launched.<ref name=Fierce>[http://www.fiercepharmamarketing.com/story/amgen-slaps-record-breaking-178k-price-rare-leukemia-drug-blincyto/2014-12-18 Amgen slaps record-breaking $178K price on rare leukemia drug Blincyto]</ref>

==Mechanism of action==

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]] located on lymphocytes.  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response to cancer cells which express [[PD-L1|programmed death-ligand]] (PD-L1 or PD-L2).  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In cancer patients antibody blockade against this receptor such as with Pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy.|journal=Nature reviews. Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[Cancer immunotherapy#Immune checkpoints|checkpoint therapy]].

== Clinical trials ==
As of 2015 a large phase I [[clinical trial]] produced response rates of 37–38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with Ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date=April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full |pmid=25838373 |volume=348 |pages=56–61}}</ref>

Also in 2015 the drug was in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], University of Pennsylvania, February 4, 2015.</ref>

The KEYNOTE-012 phase IB study is testing pembrolizumab for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]].<ref name=Key012-2016-05>[http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727 Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer]</ref> It reported encouraging interim results (eg in TNBC) in 2016.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/> The ongoing phase II KEYNOTE-086 study is evaluating a 200-mg dose of pembrolizumab, given once every 3 weeks, in patients with TNBC.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/>

== History ==
Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA"/> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904"/> It is marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]].

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444/><ref name="FDA2"/>

Pembrolizumab was priced at $150,000 per year when it launched (late 2014).<ref name=Fierce>[http://www.fiercepharmamarketing.com/story/amgen-slaps-record-breaking-178k-price-rare-leukemia-drug-blincyto/2014-12-18 Amgen slaps record-breaking $178K price on rare leukemia drug Blincyto]</ref>

==Mechanism of action==

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]] located on lymphocytes.  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response to cancer cells which express [[PD-L1|programmed death-ligand]] (PD-L1 or PD-L2).  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In cancer patients antibody blockade against this receptor such as with Pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy.|journal=Nature reviews. Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[Cancer immunotherapy#Immune checkpoints|checkpoint therapy]].

== Clinical trials ==
In 2015 a large phase I [[clinical trial]] produced response rates of 37–38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with Ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date=April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full |pmid=25838373 |volume=348 |pages=56–61}}</ref>

Also in 2015 the drug was in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], University of Pennsylvania, February 4, 2015.</ref>

The KEYNOTE-012 phase IB study is testing pembrolizumab for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]].<ref name=Key012-2016-05>[http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727 Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer]</ref> It reported encouraging interim results (eg in TNBC) in 2016.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/> The ongoing phase II KEYNOTE-086 study is evaluating a 200-mg dose of pembrolizumab, given once every 3 weeks, in patients with TNBC.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/>

Reported in June, 2016 a phase II clinical trial of pembrolizumab showed an objective response rate of 56% when used to treat advanced [[Merkel-cell carcinoma]].<ref name=Nghiem2016>{{cite journal |url=http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |title=PD-1 Blockade with Pembrolizumab in Advanced Merkel-Cell Carcinoma. |journal=N Engl J Med 2016; 374:2542-2552 |year=2016 |author=Nghiem et al. |doi=10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 }}</ref> These response rates were observed with virus-positive and virus-negative tumors.

== History ==
Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA"/> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904"/> It is marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]].

Pembrolizumab was priced at $150,000 per year when it launched (late 2014).<ref name=Fierce>[http://www.fiercepharmamarketing.com/story/amgen-slaps-record-breaking-178k-price-rare-leukemia-drug-blincyto/2014-12-18 Amgen slaps record-breaking $178K price on rare leukemia drug Blincyto]</ref>

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444/><ref name="FDA2"/>

{{as of|July 2016}} the US FDA is considering an application for [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC).<ref>[http://global.onclive.com/web-exclusives/potential-biomarkers-identified-for-pembrolizumab-in-head-and-neck-cancer Potential Biomarkers Identified for Pembrolizumab in Head and Neck Cancer. July 2016]</ref>

== Clinical trials ==
In 2015 a large phase I [[clinical trial]] produced response rates of 37–38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with Ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date=April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full |pmid=25838373 |volume=348 |pages=56–61}}</ref>

Also in 2015 the drug was in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], University of Pennsylvania, February 4, 2015.</ref>

The KEYNOTE-012 phase IB study is testing pembrolizumab for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]].<ref name=Key012-2016-05>[http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727 Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer]</ref> It reported encouraging interim results (eg in TNBC) in 2016.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/> The ongoing phase II KEYNOTE-086 study is evaluating a 200-mg dose of pembrolizumab, given once every 3 weeks, in patients with TNBC.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/>

Reported in June, 2016 a phase II clinical trial of pembrolizumab showed an objective response rate of 56% when used to treat advanced [[Merkel-cell carcinoma]].<ref name=Nghiem2016>{{cite journal |url=http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |title=PD-1 Blockade with Pembrolizumab in Advanced Merkel-Cell Carcinoma. |journal=N. Engl. J. Med. |volume=374 |issue= |pages=2542-2552 |year=2016 |last=Nghiem |doi=10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 }}</ref> These response rates were observed with virus-positive and virus-negative tumors.

== Approvals and indications ==
===Advanced melanoma===
On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced melanoma|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm412802.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=September 4, 2014}}</ref> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904">{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref> 

===Non-small cell lung cancer===
On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444>{{cite web|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small cell lung cancer|publisher=}}</ref><ref name="FDA2">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small-cell lung cancer|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=October 2, 2015}}</ref>

'''Head and Neck Cancer''' 

The FDA has granted an accelerated approval to pembrolizumab (Keytruda) as a treatment for patients with recurrent or metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC) following progression on a platinum-based chemotherapy, based on objective response rates (ORR) in the phase Ib KEYNOTE-012 study.<ref>[http://www.onclive.com/web-exclusives/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-for-head-and-neck-cancer?e5=8406f8fad9ee17bc2a7a0478093badb9&utm_source=Informz&utm_medium=OncLive&utm_campaign=Breaking_News_5-18-16#sthash.wIOn1EW1.dpuf]</ref>

==Mechanism of action==

Normally, T cells can destroy some cancer cells, but cancer cells evolve mechanisms to protect themselves from T cells. Pemrolizumab blocks that protective mechanism and allows T cells to destroy cancer cells again. 

Ligands are molecules that bind to receptors. PD-1 is a receptor on the surface of T cells, which binds to a ligand, PD-L1, on the surface of normal cells. The PD-L1 ligand protects normal cells from being attacked by T cells. But cancer cells also express PDL-1, which protects them from being attacked by T cells too. In cancer patients, pemrolizumab blocks PD-1, and allows T cells to attack them again. 

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]] located on lymphocytes.  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response to cancer cells which express [[PD-L1|programmed death-ligand]] (PD-L1 or PD-L2).  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In cancer patients antibody blockade against this receptor such as with Pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy|journal=Nature Reviews Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|pmc=4856023|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[Cancer immunotherapy#Immune checkpoints|checkpoint therapy]].

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444/><ref name="FDA2"/>

{{as of|July 2016}} the US FDA is considering an application for [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC).<ref>[http://global.onclive.com/web-exclusives/potential-biomarkers-identified-for-pembrolizumab-in-head-and-neck-cancer Potential Biomarkers Identified for Pembrolizumab in Head and Neck Cancer. July 2016]</ref>

== Clinical trials ==
In 2015 a large phase I [[clinical trial]] produced response rates of 37–38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with Ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date=April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full |pmid=25838373 |volume=348 |issue=6230 |pages=56–61|bibcode=2015Sci...348...56S }}</ref>

Also in 2015 the drug was in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], University of Pennsylvania, February 4, 2015.</ref>

The KEYNOTE-012 phase IB study is testing pembrolizumab for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]].<ref name=Key012-2016-05>[http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727 Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer]</ref> It reported encouraging interim results (eg in TNBC) in 2016.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/> The ongoing phase II KEYNOTE-086 study is evaluating a 200-mg dose of pembrolizumab, given once every 3 weeks, in patients with TNBC.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/>

Reported in June, 2016 a phase II clinical trial of pembrolizumab showed an objective response rate of 56% when used to treat advanced [[Merkel-cell carcinoma]].<ref name=Nghiem2016>{{cite journal |url=http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |title=PD-1 Blockade with Pembrolizumab in Advanced Merkel-Cell Carcinoma |journal=N. Engl. J. Med. |volume=374 |issue= 26|pages=2542–2552 |year=2016 |last=Nghiem |doi=10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |pmid=27093365 |pmc=4927341 }}</ref> These response rates were observed with virus-positive and virus-negative tumors.

== Approvals and indications ==
===Advanced melanoma===
On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced melanoma|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm412802.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=September 4, 2014}}</ref> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904">{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref> 

===Non-small cell lung cancer===
On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444>{{cite web|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small cell lung cancer|publisher=}}</ref><ref name="FDA2">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small-cell lung cancer|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=October 2, 2015}}</ref>

===Head and Neck Cancer===

In August 2016 the FDA granted an [[accelerated approval]] to pembrolizumab (Keytruda) as a treatment for patients with recurrent or metastatic [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC) following progression on a platinum-based chemotherapy, based on [[objective response rate]]s (ORR) in the phase Ib KEYNOTE-012 study.<ref name=HNSCC-AA>[http://www.onclive.com/web-exclusives/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-for-head-and-neck-cancer?e5=8406f8fad9ee17bc2a7a0478093badb9&utm_source=Informz&utm_medium=OncLive&utm_campaign=Breaking_News_5-18-16#sthash.wIOn1EW1.dpuf]</ref>

==Mechanism of action==

Normally, T cells can destroy some cancer cells, but cancer cells evolve mechanisms to protect themselves from T cells. Pembrolizumab blocks that protective mechanism and allows T cells to destroy cancer cells again. 

Ligands are molecules that bind to receptors. PD-1 is a receptor on the surface of T cells, which binds to a ligand, PD-L1, on the surface of normal cells. The PD-L1 ligand protects normal cells from being attacked by T cells. But cancer cells also express PD-L1, which protects them from being attacked by T cells too. In cancer patients, pembrolizumab binds to PD-1 on T cells and blocks association with PD-L1, thereby allowing T cells to attack the cancer. 

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]] located on lymphocytes.  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response to cancer cells which express [[PD-L1|programmed death-ligand]] (PD-L1 or PD-L2).  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In cancer patients antibody blockade against this receptor such as with Pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy|journal=Nature Reviews Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|pmc=4856023|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[Cancer immunotherapy#Immune checkpoints|checkpoint therapy]].

== History ==
Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA"/> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904"/> It is marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]].

Pembrolizumab was priced at $150,000 per year when it launched (late 2014).<ref name=Fierce>[http://www.fiercepharmamarketing.com/story/amgen-slaps-record-breaking-178k-price-rare-leukemia-drug-blincyto/2014-12-18 Amgen slaps record-breaking $178K price on rare leukemia drug Blincyto]</ref>

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444/><ref name="FDA2"/>

{{as of|July 2016}} the US FDA accepted for priority review an application for recurrent or metastatic [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC) after a platinum-based chemotherapy,<ref>[http://global.onclive.com/web-exclusives/potential-biomarkers-identified-for-pembrolizumab-in-head-and-neck-cancer Potential Biomarkers Identified for Pembrolizumab in Head and Neck Cancer. July 2016]</ref> and gave an accelerated approval on August 9, 2016.<ref name=HNSCC-AA/>

The KEYNOTE-012 phase IB study is testing pembrolizumab for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]].<ref name=Key012-2016-05>[http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727 Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer]</ref> It reported encouraging interim results (eg in TNBC) in 2016.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/> The ongoing phase II KEYNOTE-086 study is evaluating a 200-mg dose of pembrolizumab, given once every 3 weeks, in patients with TNBC.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/>

Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA"/> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904"/> It is marketed by [[Merck & Co.|Merck]].

Pembrolizumab was priced at $150,000 per year when it launched (late 2014).<ref name=Fierce>[http://www.fiercepharmamarketing.com/story/amgen-slaps-record-breaking-178k-price-rare-leukemia-drug-blincyto/2014-12-18 Amgen slaps record-breaking $178K price on rare leukemia drug Blincyto]</ref>

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444/><ref name="FDA2"/>

In July 2016 the US FDA accepted for priority review an application for recurrent or metastatic [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC) after a platinum-based chemotherapy,<ref>[http://global.onclive.com/web-exclusives/potential-biomarkers-identified-for-pembrolizumab-in-head-and-neck-cancer Potential Biomarkers Identified for Pembrolizumab in Head and Neck Cancer. July 2016]</ref> and gave an accelerated approval on August 9, 2016.<ref name=HNSCC-AA/>

== Clinical trials ==
In 2015 a large phase I [[clinical trial]] produced response rates of 37–38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with Ipilimumab.<ref name=sa15>{{cite journal |title=The future of immune checkpoint therapy |journal=Science |date=April 3, 2015 |accessdate=June 2015 |last1=Sharma |first1=Pamanee |last2=Allison |first2=James P. |doi=10.1126/science.aaa8172 |url=http://www.sciencemag.org/content/348/6230/56.full |pmid=25838373 |volume=348 |issue=6230 |pages=56–61|bibcode=2015Sci...348...56S }}</ref>

Also in 2015 the drug was in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.<ref>Press release, [http://www.upenn.edu/pennnews/news/penn-medicines-new-immunotherapy-study-will-pit-pd-1-inhibitor-against-advanced-lung-cancer "Penn Medicine's New Immunotherapy Study Will Pit PD-1 Inhibitor Against Advanced Lung Cancer"], University of Pennsylvania, February 4, 2015.</ref>

The KEYNOTE-012 phase IB study is testing pembrolizumab for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]].<ref name=Key012-2016-05>[http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727 Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer]</ref> It reported encouraging interim results (eg in TNBC) in 2016.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/> The ongoing phase II KEYNOTE-086 study is evaluating a 200-mg dose of pembrolizumab, given once every 3 weeks, in patients with TNBC.<ref name=Key012-2016-05/>

Reported in June, 2016 a phase II clinical trial of pembrolizumab showed an objective response rate of 56% when used to treat advanced [[Merkel-cell carcinoma]].<ref name=Nghiem2016>{{cite journal |url=http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |title=PD-1 Blockade with Pembrolizumab in Advanced Merkel-Cell Carcinoma |journal=N. Engl. J. Med. |volume=374 |issue= 26|pages=2542–2552 |year=2016 |last=Nghiem |doi=10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |pmid=27093365 |pmc=4927341 }}</ref> These response rates were observed with virus-positive and virus-negative tumors.

== Medical uses ==
== Contraindications ==
== Adverse effects ==
== Interactions ==
== Pharmacology ==
Normally, T cells can destroy some cancer cells, but cancer cells evolve mechanisms to protect themselves from T cells. Pembrolizumab blocks that protective mechanism and allows T cells to destroy cancer cells again. 

Ligands are molecules that bind to receptors. PD-1 is a receptor on the surface of T cells, which binds to a ligand, PD-L1, on the surface of normal cells. The PD-L1 ligand protects normal cells from being attacked by T cells. But cancer cells also express PD-L1, which protects them from being attacked by T cells too. In cancer patients, pembrolizumab binds to PD-1 on T cells and blocks association with PD-L1, thereby allowing T cells to attack the cancer. 

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]] located on lymphocytes.  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response to cancer cells which express [[PD-L1|programmed death-ligand]] (PD-L1 or PD-L2).  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In cancer patients antibody blockade against this receptor such as with Pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy|journal=Nature Reviews Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|pmc=4856023|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[Cancer immunotherapy#Immune checkpoints|checkpoint therapy]].

== History ==
Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced melanoma|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm412802.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=September 4, 2014}}</ref> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904">{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref> 

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444>{{cite web|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small cell lung cancer|publisher=}}</ref><ref name="FDA2">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small-cell lung cancer|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=October 2, 2015}}</ref>

In July 2016 the US FDA accepted for priority review an application for recurrent or metastatic [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC) after a platinum-based chemotherapy,<ref>[http://global.onclive.com/web-exclusives/potential-biomarkers-identified-for-pembrolizumab-in-head-and-neck-cancer Potential Biomarkers Identified for Pembrolizumab in Head and Neck Cancer. July 2016]</ref> and gave an accelerated approval on August 9, 2016.<ref name=HNSCC-AA/>

In August 2016 the FDA granted an [[accelerated approval]] to pembrolizumab (Keytruda) as a treatment for patients with recurrent or metastatic [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC) following progression on a platinum-based chemotherapy, based on [[objective response rate]]s (ORR) in the phase Ib KEYNOTE-012 study.<ref name=HNSCC-AA>[http://www.onclive.com/web-exclusives/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-for-head-and-neck-cancer?e5=8406f8fad9ee17bc2a7a0478093badb9&utm_source=Informz&utm_medium=OncLive&utm_campaign=Breaking_News_5-18-16#sthash.wIOn1EW1.dpuf]</ref>

In 2015 a large phase I [[clinical trial]] produced response rates of 37–38% in patients with advanced melanoma and an overall response rate of 26% in patients who had progressive disease after treatment with Ipilimumab.{{cn}}

Also in 2015 the drug was in Phase II clinical trials for [[non-small-cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients with oligometastatic disease.{{cn}}

As of May 2016 pembrolizumab was in Phase IB clinical trials for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]] (all under the "Keynote-012" trial)  and in Phase II trial for TNBC (the "Keynote-086" trial).<ref name=Key012-2016-05>{{cite news|last1=Jenkins|first1=Kristin|title=Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer|url=http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727|work=MedPage Today|date=5 May 2016}}</ref>

Results of a Phase II clinical trial in [[Merkel-cell carcinoma]] were reported in June 2016.<ref name=Nghiem2016>{{cite journal |url=http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |title=PD-1 Blockade with Pembrolizumab in Advanced Merkel-Cell Carcinoma |journal=N. Engl. J. Med. |volume=374 |issue= 26|pages=2542–2552 |year=2016 |last=Nghiem |doi=10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |pmid=27093365 |pmc=4927341 }}</ref> These response rates were observed with virus-positive and virus-negative tumors.

== Medical uses ==
As of 2016, pembrolizumab is used to treat inoperable or metastatic melanoma, metastatic [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in certain situations, and as a second-line treatment for head and neck small-cell cancer]], after platinum-based chemotherapy.<ref name=USlabel2016>{{cite web|title=Pembrolizumab label|url=http://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2016/125514s012lbl.pdf|publisher=FDA|date=October 2016}} linked from [http://www.accessdata.fda.gov/scripts/cder/daf/index.cfm?event=overview.process&varApplNo=125514 Index page at FDA website] November 2016</ref><ref name=UKlabel2016>{{cite web|title=Pembrolizumab label at eMC|url=https://www.medicines.org.uk/emc/medicine/30602|publisher=UK Electronic Medicines Compendium|date=18 August 2016}}</ref>  For NSCLC, pembrolizumab is a first line treatment if the cancer overexpresses PDL1 and the cancer has no mutations in [[EGFR]] or in [[ALK]]; is chemotherapy has already been administered, then pembrolizumab can be used a second line treatment but if the cancer has EGFR or ALK mutations, agents targeting those mutations should be used first.<ref name=USlabel2016/>

== Contraindications ==
== Adverse effects ==
== Interactions ==
== Pharmacology ==
Normally, T cells can destroy some cancer cells, but cancer cells evolve mechanisms to protect themselves from T cells. Pembrolizumab blocks that protective mechanism and allows T cells to destroy cancer cells again. 

Ligands are molecules that bind to receptors. PD-1 is a receptor on the surface of T cells, which binds to a ligand, PD-L1, on the surface of normal cells. The PD-L1 ligand protects normal cells from being attacked by T cells. But cancer cells also express PD-L1, which protects them from being attacked by T cells too. In cancer patients, pembrolizumab binds to PD-1 on T cells and blocks association with PD-L1, thereby allowing T cells to attack the cancer. 

Pembrolizumab is a [[therapeutic antibody]] that blocks the inhibitory ligand of [[PD-1|programmed cell death 1 receptor]] located on lymphocytes.  This receptor is responsible for inhibiting the immune response to cancer cells which express [[PD-L1|programmed death-ligand]] (PD-L1 or PD-L2).  Normally, this effect is necessary to avoid inappropriate overreaction, such as an [[auto-immune disease]], in healthy individuals.<ref name="pmid20636820">{{cite journal | vauthors = Francisco LM, Sage PT, Sharpe AH | title = The PD-1 pathway in tolerance and autoimmunity | journal = Immunological Reviews | volume = 236 | issue =  | pages = 219–42 | date = Jul 2010 | pmid = 20636820 | pmc = 2919275 | doi = 10.1111/j.1600-065X.2010.00923.x }}</ref> In cancer patients antibody blockade against this receptor such as with Pembrolizumab reinvigorates the immune system, allowing it to target and destroy cancer cells.<ref name=Pardoll2012>{{cite journal|last=Pardoll|first=DM|title=The blockade of immune checkpoints in cancer immunotherapy|journal=Nature Reviews Cancer|date=Mar 22, 2012|volume=12|issue=4|pages=252–64|pmid=22437870|pmc=4856023|doi=10.1038/nrc3239}}</ref> Pembrolizumab is one of a number of closely related therapies dubbed [[Cancer immunotherapy#Immune checkpoints|checkpoint therapy]].

== Chemistry and manufacturing ==

== History ==
Pembrolizumab was invented by Gregory Carven, Hans van Eenennaam and John Dulos at Organon Biosciences.<ref>{{patent|US|8952136|Antibodies to human programmed death receptor PD-1 }}</ref> MRC Technology humanized the antibody pembrolizumab for Organon in 2006.

On September 4, 2014 the US [[Food and Drug Administration]] ([[FDA]]) approved pembrolizumab under the [[FDA Fast Track Development Program]].<ref name="FDA">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced melanoma|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm412802.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=September 4, 2014}}</ref> It is approved for use following treatment with [[ipilimumab]], or after treatment with Ipilimumab and a [[BRAF inhibitor]] in advanced [[melanoma]] patients who carry a [[BRAF (gene)|BRAF]] mutation.<ref name="FDA20140904">{{cite web|url=http://www.cancernetwork.com/news/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-keytruda-advanced-melanoma|title=FDA Approves Anti-PD-1 Drug for Advanced Melanoma|work=cancernetwork.com}}</ref> 

On October 2, 2015, the FDA approved pembrolizumab for the treatment of [[metastatic]] [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors express [[PD-L1]] and who have failed treatment with other [[chemotherapeutic]] agents.<ref name=FDA-ucm465444>{{cite web|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small cell lung cancer|publisher=}}</ref><ref name="FDA2">{{cite news|last1=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|title=FDA approves Keytruda for advanced non-small-cell lung cancer|url=http://www.fda.gov/NewsEvents/Newsroom/PressAnnouncements/ucm465444.htm|accessdate=24 December 2015|agency=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|publisher=U.S. Food and Drug Administration|date=October 2, 2015}}</ref>

In July 2016 the US FDA accepted for priority review an application for recurrent or metastatic [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC) after a platinum-based chemotherapy,<ref>[http://global.onclive.com/web-exclusives/potential-biomarkers-identified-for-pembrolizumab-in-head-and-neck-cancer Potential Biomarkers Identified for Pembrolizumab in Head and Neck Cancer. July 2016]</ref> and gave an accelerated approval on August 9, 2016.<ref name=HNSCC-AA/>

In August 2016 the FDA granted an [[accelerated approval]] to pembrolizumab (Keytruda) as a treatment for patients with recurrent or metastatic [[head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]] (HNSCC) following progression on a platinum-based chemotherapy, based on [[objective response rate]]s (ORR) in the phase Ib KEYNOTE-012 study.<ref name=HNSCC-AA>[http://www.onclive.com/web-exclusives/fda-approves-pembrolizumab-for-head-and-neck-cancer?e5=8406f8fad9ee17bc2a7a0478093badb9&utm_source=Informz&utm_medium=OncLive&utm_campaign=Breaking_News_5-18-16#sthash.wIOn1EW1.dpuf]</ref>

== Society and culture ==
Pembrolizumab was priced at $150,000 per year when it launched (late 2014).<ref name=Fierce>[http://www.fiercepharmamarketing.com/story/amgen-slaps-record-breaking-178k-price-rare-leukemia-drug-blincyto/2014-12-18 Amgen slaps record-breaking $178K price on rare leukemia drug Blincyto]</ref>

== Research ==

In 2015 Merck reported results in 13 cancer types; much attention was given to early results in [[head and neck cancer]].<ref>{{cite news|last1=Timmerman|first1=Luke|title=ASCO Wrapup: Immunotherapy Shines, Hope For Brain Tumors, & The Great Cancer Drug Price Debate|url=http://www.forbes.com/sites/luketimmerman/2015/06/02/asco-wrapup-immunotherapy-shines-hope-for-brain-tumors-the-great-cancer-drug-price-debate/#24560e251f9b|work=Forbes|date=June 2, 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last1=Adams|first1=Katherine T.|title=Cancer Immunotherapies--and Their Cost--Take Center Stage at ASCO's 2015 Annual Meeting|url=http://www.managedcaremag.com/archives/2015/7/cancer-immunotherapies-and-their-cost-take-center-stage-ascos-2015-annual-meeting|work=Managed Care Magazine Online|date=24 July 2015}}</ref>

As of May 2016 pembrolizumab was in Phase IB clinical trials for [[triple-negative breast cancer]] (TNBC), [[gastric cancer]], [[urothelial cancer]], and [[head and neck cancer]] (all under the "Keynote-012" trial)  and in Phase II trial for TNBC (the "Keynote-086" trial).<ref name=Key012-2016-05>{{cite news|last1=Jenkins|first1=Kristin|title=Keytruda Impresses in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer|url=http://www.medpagetoday.com/HematologyOncology/BreastCancer/57727|work=MedPage Today|date=5 May 2016}}</ref>  At ASCO in June 2016 Merck reported that the clinical development program was directed to around 30 cancers and that it was running over 270 clinical trials (around 100 in combination with other treatments) and had four registration-enabling studies in process.<ref>{{cite news|title=Merck & Co updates Keytruda findings at ASCO|url=http://www.thepharmaletter.com/article/merck-co-updates-keytruda-findings-at-asco|work=The Pharma Letter|date=June 10, 2016}}</ref>

Results of a Phase II clinical trial in [[Merkel-cell carcinoma]] were reported in the New England Journal of Medicine in June 2016.<ref name=Nghiem2016>{{cite journal |url=http://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |title=PD-1 Blockade with Pembrolizumab in Advanced Merkel-Cell Carcinoma |journal=N. Engl. J. Med. |volume=374 |issue= 26|pages=2542–2552 |year=2016 |last=Nghiem |doi=10.1056/NEJMoa1603702 |pmid=27093365 |pmc=4927341 }}</ref>

== Medical uses ==
As of 2016, pembrolizumab is used to treat inoperable or metastatic melanoma, metastatic [[non-small cell lung cancer]] (NSCLC) in certain situations, and as a second-line treatment for [[ head and neck squamous cell carcinoma]], after platinum-based chemotherapy.<ref name=USlabel2016>{{cite web|title=Pembrolizumab label|url=http://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2016/125514s012lbl.pdf|publisher=FDA|date=October 2016}} linked from [http://www.accessdata.fda.gov/scripts/cder/daf/index.cfm?event=overview.process&varApplNo=125514 Index page at FDA website] November 2016</ref><ref name=UKlabel2016>{{cite web|title=Pembrolizumab label at eMC|url=https://www.medicines.org.uk/emc/medicine/30602|publisher=UK Electronic Medicines Compendium|date=18 August 2016}}</ref>  For NSCLC, pembrolizumab is a first line treatment if the cancer overexpresses PDL1 and the cancer has no mutations in [[EGFR]] or in [[ALK]]; if chemotherapy has already been administered, then pembrolizumab can be used a second line treatment but if the cancer has EGFR or ALK mutations, agents targeting those mutations should be used first.<ref name=USlabel2016/>

Women of child-bearing age should use contraception when taking pembrolizumab; it should not be administered to pregnant women because animals studies have shown that it can reduce tolerance to the fetus and increase miscarriages.  It is not known if pembrolizumab is secreted in breast milk or not.<ref name=UKlabel2016/>

== Contraindications ==
If a person is taking corticosteroids or immunosuppressants those drugs should be stopped before starting pembrolizumab because they may interfere with pembrolizumab; they can be used after pembrolizumab is started to deal with immune-related adverse effects.<ref name=UKlabel2016/>

== Adverse effects ==
== Interactions ==

The PD-L1 ligand protects normal cells from being attacked by T cells. But cancer cells also express PD-L1, which protects them from being attacked by T cells too. In cancer patients, pembrolizumab binds to PD-1 on T cells and blocks association with PD-L1, thereby allowing T cells to attack the cancer.

== Chemistry and manufacturing ==
Pembrolizumab is an Immunoglobulin G4, with a variable region against human [[Programmed cell death 1]], a humanized mouse monoclonal [228-L-proline(H10-S>P)]γ4 heavy chain (134-218') disulfide and a humanized mouse monoclonal κ light chain dimer (226-226'':229-229'')-bisdisulfide.<ref>{{cite document | url = https://searchusan.ama-assn.org/usan/documentDownload?uri=%2Funstructured%2Fbinary%2Fusan%2Fpembrolizumab.pdf | title = Statement on a Nonproprietary Name Adopted by the USAN Council | date = November 27, 2013}}</ref>

It is recombinantly manufactured in [[Chinese hamster ovary]] (CHO) cells.<ref>{{cite web|title=Assessment report: Keytruda. Procedure No. EMEA/H/C/003820/0000|url=http://www.ema.europa.eu/docs/en_GB/document_library/EPAR_-_Public_assessment_report/human/003820/WC500190992.pdf|publisher=EMA|date=21 May 2015}}</ref>

'''Pembrolizumab''' (formerly '''MK-3475''' and '''lambrolizumab''', trade name '''Keytruda''')<ref>{{cite web|title=Pembrolizumab|url=http://adisinsight.springer.com/drugs/800034086|publisher=AdisInsight|accessdate=5 November 2016}}</ref> is a [[humanized antibody]] used in [[cancer immunotherapy]]. It destroys a protective mechanism on cancer cells, and allows the immune system to destroy those cancer cells.
